title: Spermacoce thalictroides
taxonomy: Spermacoce thalictroides is classified in the kingdom Plantae, and more specifically, in the family Rubiaceae. This species belongs to the order Gentianales and is a part of the clade angiosperms. Within the family Rubiaceae, Spermacoce thalictroides is placed in the subfamily Rubioideae. At the genus level, Spermacoce thalictroides is a member of the genus Spermacoce. This classification helps to group the plant with its closest relatives and understand its evolutionary relationships.
uses: Spermacoce thalictroides, also known as Wood's bugleweed, has been traditionally used in folk medicine for its potential antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties. The plant is said to have been used to treat a variety of ailments, including wounds, fever, and respiratory issues. In some parts of the world, Spermacoce thalictroides is used as a poultice to reduce swelling and promote healing. However, more research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of using the plant for medicinal purposes. Its potential medicinal uses should be approached with caution and under the guidance of a qualified healthcare professional.
benefits: Spermacoce thalictroides, also known as Wood's rose or Wood's gentian, has been found to have potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. The plant is traditionally used in herbal medicine to treat various ailments, including fever, colds, and respiratory issues. Its leaves and stems are said to have antiseptic and antifungal properties, making it effective in treating wounds and skin infections. However, further research is necessary to confirm the efficacy and safety of using Spermacoce thalictroides for medicinal purposes. More studies are required to fully understand its potential health benefits and potential side effects.

These compounds are thought to be responsible for the plant's medicinal properties. Flavonoids, in particular, have been shown to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects.
